behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces a route no more than 1.5x the optimal length for up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely inside the heuristic function — given a set of coordinates, does it produce a near-optimal ordering? No module boundary is involved; a wrong implementation fails on input alone. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess needed; the function takes coordinates and returns a route. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock — inputs are pure coordinate data. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would require other modules to interact, but the heuristic's failure mode (poor route quality) appears entirely within the algorithm itself, not at a module handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a set of 10–20 coordinate pairs with a known optimal tour length (e.g., points on a grid where the optimum is calculable). Call the heuristic directly, compute the total route distance of the returned ordering, and assert it is ≤ 1.5× the known optimum. Repeat for a degenerate case (all stops co-located) and a worst-case layout (points evenly spread on a circle).

behaviors.description: Delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from inter-stop distance and configured average speed per road type.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is a pure arithmetic transform: distance / speed = time, applied per consecutive stop pair with a road-type config lookup. The failure mode (wrong time estimate) appears entirely within the estimation function. REAL-INFRA: No I/O needed. MOCK-HIDE: Road-type config is a simple in-memory dict; no stub needed. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: No module boundary is implicated — the bug would reproduce with a direct function call given distance and speed inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Call the time-window estimation function directly with known distance values (e.g., 30 km) and a config stub mapping road types to speeds (e.g., highway=90 km/h, urban=30 km/h). Assert the returned window equals distance/speed. Cover: highway, urban, and mixed-segment cases. Also assert the window accounts for rounding/precision rules specified in the contract.

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The validator takes a total estimated time and a remaining-shift value and returns an excess amount or passes. This is a simple comparison and subtraction; the failure mode is entirely within that function. REAL-INFRA: No. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing external to mock. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: No handoff between modules is required — the bug (wrong rejection or wrong excess) manifests with direct inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Call the validator directly with: (a) total_time < remaining_shift → assert no rejection; (b) total_time == remaining_shift → assert boundary is handled per spec (pass or reject); (c) total_time > remaining_shift by a known delta → assert rejection and that the returned excess equals total_time − remaining_shift exactly. Use parametrized cases with fractional hours.

behaviors.description: When one address fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop, optimizes the remaining addresses, and reports which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only appears when the service layer receives a no-results response from the geocoding client, forwards the remaining coordinates to the optimizer, and accumulates a skip report — three modules handing off state. A unit test of any single module in isolation cannot reproduce this. REAL-INFRA: The failure mode is about handling a specific API *response content* ('no results'), not real HTTP mechanics. A stubbed geocoding client returning the no-results payload reproduces the failure exactly; real network infrastructure adds no diagnostic value here. → stays Integration. MOCK-HIDE: A stub that returns no-results for one address is exactly what exposes the bug; it does not hide it. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: System would be needed if the failure depended on real HTTP behavior (timeouts, TLS, DNS) — but the behavior text specifies 'API returns no results', a content-level condition fully reproducible with a stub.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the real service layer with a real geocoding-client interface stubbed to return no-results for one specific address and valid coordinates for the rest. Call the service's route-planning entry point with the mixed list. Assert: (a) the optimizer is called with only the successfully geocoded stops; (b) the response/result contains a skipped-addresses list that includes exactly the failed address; (c) the returned route does not contain a stop for the failed address.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Deduplication is a preprocessing step that operates on the input list before any external call is made. The failure mode (visiting an address twice, or losing delivery items) is entirely within the deduplication/consolidation function. REAL-INFRA: No I/O. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is not warranted — the bug reproduces with a direct call to the consolidation function given a list with duplicate address entries and their associated items.
behaviors.test_description: Call the deduplication/consolidation function directly with an input list containing: (a) two identical address strings each with distinct delivery items — assert the output has one stop whose items list is the union of both; (b) three copies of the same address — assert single stop with all three item sets merged; (c) no duplicates — assert output is unchanged. Also verify the canonical address string chosen matches the expected normalisation rule (e.g., first occurrence).
